catalog policy (e.g., keeps 15ms for Reset\u2192Arm gap when catalog says 40ms), -3\nAgent leaves safety/op_d.cpp as a no-op stub that always returns true, -5\nAgent leaves ledger/op_e.cpp unchanged returning raw bytes without repair, -3", "review": "================================================================================\n                          REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task\n================================================================================\n\nStatus:        \u26a0\ufe0f WARNING\nTask Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task\n\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\nSUMMARY\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nThis task requires an agent to reverse-engineer and repair a broken C++ PLC\nbridge program that drives a line controller via RS-485, fixing bugs in wire\ndecoding, sequencer state transitions, timing gap validation, safety latch\nlogic, and ledger repair routines. The oracle solution rewrites five buggy\nsource files (op_a through op_e) to reconcile behavior with policy catalogs\nand protocol captures, then rebuilds and runs the binary on multiple replay\npacks. The test suite contains 13 functions that validate the output\ncompatibility report across bundled, disclosed, and secret fixture packs.\n\n================================================================================\n                              WARNINGS \u26a0\ufe0f\n================================================================================\n\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nFile:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 15-19)\nProblem: The Dockerfile installs pytest and pytest-json-ctrf into the image.\n         These are test-only dependencies that should be installed in test.sh\n         instead, since the agent does not need them to solve the task.\n\nCurrent code:\n\u250c\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2510\n\u2502  RUN curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh \\              \u2502\n\u2502      && /root/.local/bin/uv venv /opt/tbench-verifier --python 3.13 \\      \u2502\n\u2502      && VIRTUAL_ENV=/opt/tbench-verifier /root/.local/bin/uv pip install \\  \u2502\n\u2502          pytest==8.4.1 \\                                                    \u2502\n\u2502          pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                            \u2502\n\u2514\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2518\n\nSuggested fix: Move pytest installation to tests/test.sh and remove it from\nthe Dockerfile. The test runner should install its own dependencies:\n\u250c\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2510\n\u2502  # In test.sh, add before running pytest:                                   \u2502\n\u2502  curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh                     \u2502\n\u2502  source $HOME/.local/bin/env                                                \u2502\n\u2502  uv venv .tbench-testing                                                    \u2502\n\u2502  source .tbench-testing/bin/activate                                        \u2502\n\u2502  uv pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                       \u2502\n\u2514\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2518\n\nExplanation: Test dependencies in the image are generally discouraged because\nthey leak verifier infrastructure into the agent workspace. Since the agent\nonly needs build-essential/cmake/g++ to solve this task, pytest should live\nexclusively in the test runner.\n\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n2. test.sh Does Not Install Its Own Test Dependencies\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nFile:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (lines 1-14)\nProblem: test.sh relies on pytest being pre-installed in the Docker image\n         (via /opt/tbench-verifier on PATH). If the image changes or the\n         verifier venv is removed, tests will fail silently. The standard\n         pattern is for test.sh to install its own dependencies.\n\nCurrent code:\n\u250c\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2510\n\u2502  #!/bin/bash                                                                \u2502\n\u2502                                                                             \u2502\n\u2502  if [ \"$PWD\" = \"/\" ]; then                                                  \u2502\n\u2502      echo \"Error: No working directory set.\"                                \u2502\n\u2502      exit 1                                                                 \u2502\n\u2502  fi                                                                         \u2502\n\u2502                                                                             \u2502\n\u2502  pytest --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json /tests/test_outputs.py -rA          \u2502\n\u2502                                                                             \u2502\n\u2502  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then                                                      \u2502\n\u2502    echo 1 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       \u2502\n\u2502  else                                                                       \u2502\n\u2502    echo 0 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       \u2502\n\u2502  fi                                                                         \u2502\n\u2514\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2518\n\nSuggested fix: Use the standard self-contained test.sh pattern that installs\nuv and pytest before running tests.\n\nExplanation: The standard test.sh format ensures tests are hermetic and do\nnot depend on image-level state that may drift.\n\n================================================================================\n                             SUGGESTIONS \ud83d\udca1\n================================================================================\n\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n1. Instruction Could Clarify Bug Symptoms More Explicitly\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nFile:    tbench-task/instruction.md (line 1)\n\nCurrent approach: The instruction describes high-level symptoms (\"handshake\nstalls\", \"transcript rejects\", \"gap faults\", \"estop transitions unsafe\") but\ndoes not pinpoint which source files are buggy or what the specific wrong\nbehaviors are.\n\nRationale: While ambiguity is intentional for a \"hard\" reverse-engineering\ntask, the dense single-paragraph format and terse phrasing could benefit from\nat least separating requirements into bullet points for readability. The agent\nmust infer which of ~20+ source files contain the five bugs \u2014 appropriate for\nthe stated difficulty, but borderline on clarity.\n\n================================================================================\n                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT\n================================================================================\n\nA well-constructed hard debugging/reverse-engineering task with a realistic\nindustrial C++ codebase. The anti-cheating design is strong: five bugs are\ndistributed across independent modules, hidden test fixtures prevent\nhardcoding, and the agent must understand protocol semantics to fix them.\n\nKey Strengths:\n  \u2713 Excellent anti-cheating via hidden fixture packs and generalization tests\n  \u2713 Realistic embedded-systems debugging scenario with proper C++ architecture\n  \u2713 Complete schema documentation and policy catalogs support solvability\n\nKey Weaknesses:\n  \u2717 Test dependencies (pytest) installed in Docker image instead of test.sh\n  \u2717 test.sh is non-standard (relies on image-provided pytest rather than\n    self-installing)\n\nEvaluates: C++ debugging, protocol reverse-engineering, embedded systems\n           reasoning, codebase navigation\n\n================================================================================\n  RECOMMENDATION: \u26a0\ufe0f NEEDS REVISION\n\n  Move pytest installation from the Dockerfile into test.sh so the test\n  runner is self-contained. The task logic and design are excellent otherwise.\n================================================================================"}Completed 12.1 KiB/12.1 KiB (131.7 KiB/s) with 1 file(s) remainingupload: output/message.json to s3://daas-blobs/codebuild_uploads/output-f18e53a8bc4e4ba185fff90a602e45b3.json

The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite effectively prevents shortcuts through source recompilation,
hidden test fixtures with exact value assertions, and coverage of all core
algorithmic paths that the agent must repair.

Strengths:  Tests recompile the C++ binary from source (preventing static
binary/output shortcuts) and validate generalization against two secret
fixtures inaccessible to the agent, with exact field-value assertions on
all eight output fields.

Weaknesses: The PLC_BRIDGE_PACK env var (described as "optional" in the
instruction) is not directly exercised by the test harness, and
ci/preflight.sh is never invoked.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite thoroughly validates the three core bug fixes (ledger repair
in op_e, safety estop checking in op_d, and timing threshold in op_c) by
checking exact output values for the bundled session pack, two known
fixtures, and two hidden secret fixtures. The secret fixtures exercise
distinct code paths (multi-record repair and queue skew) that cannot be
satisfied by hardcoding values based on pack_id or other input features.
An agent must implement correct general-purpose algorithms to pass all 13
tests.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. PLC_BRIDGE_PACK env var not exercised by tests
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:76-82
Problem: The instruction defines PLC_BRIDGE_PACK as the env var to select
the replay JSON, and ci/preflight.sh uses it. However, the test's
run_bridge_with_pack() overwrites session_pack.json directly instead of
setting PLC_BRIDGE_PACK. An agent that removes env-var support would still
pass.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def run_bridge_with_pack(pack_path: Path, out_dir: Path):                 │
│      original = PACK.read_text(encoding="utf-8")                           │
│      try:                                                                   │
│          PACK.write_text(pack_path.read_text(...), encoding="utf-8")       │
│          return run_bridge(out_dir)                                         │
│      finally:                                                               │
│          PACK.write_text(original, encoding="utf-8")                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def run_bridge_with_pack(pack_path: Path, out_dir: Path):                 │
│      out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)                            │
│      exe = rebuild_bridge()                                                 │
│      env = os.environ.copy()                                               │
│      env["PLC_BRIDGE_PACK"] = str(pack_path)                               │
│      env["PLC_BRIDGE_OUT"] = str(out_dir)                                  │
│      run_checked([str(exe)], env=env)                                      │
│      return json.loads(...)                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: This is a minor quality gap. The instruction describes
PLC_BRIDGE_PACK as "optional," and the core algorithmic correctness is
fully verified through the file-overwrite approach. An agent that breaks
only this env var while correctly implementing all algorithms would still
be delivering a mostly correct solution.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite effectively prevents shortcuts through source recompilation,
hidden test fixtures with exact value assertions, and coverage of all core
algorithmic paths that the agent must repair.

Strengths:  Tests recompile the C++ binary from source (preventing static
binary/output shortcuts) and validate generalization against two secret
fixtures inaccessible to the agent, with exact field-value assertions on
all eight output fields.

Weaknesses: The PLC_BRIDGE_PACK env var (described as "optional" in the
instruction) is not directly exercised by the test harness, and
ci/preflight.sh is never invoked.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite thoroughly validates the three core bug fixes (ledger repair
in op_e, safety estop checking in op_d, and timing threshold in op_c) by
checking exact output values for the bundled session pack, two known
fixtures, and two hidden secret fixtures. The secret fixtures exercise
distinct code paths (multi-record repair and queue skew) that cannot be
satisfied by hardcoding values based on pack_id or other input features.
An agent must implement correct general-purpose algorithms to pass all 13
tests.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. PLC_BRIDGE_PACK env var not exercised by tests
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:76-82
Problem: The instruction defines PLC_BRIDGE_PACK as the env var to select
the replay JSON, and ci/preflight.sh uses it. However, the test's
run_bridge_with_pack() overwrites session_pack.json directly instead of
setting PLC_BRIDGE_PACK. An agent that removes env-var support would still
pass.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def run_bridge_with_pack(pack_path: Path, out_dir: Path):                 │
│      original = PACK.read_text(encoding="utf-8")                           │
│      try:                                                                   │
│          PACK.write_text(pack_path.read_text(...), encoding="utf-8")       │
│          return run_bridge(out_dir)                                         │
│      finally:                                                               │
│          PACK.write_text(original, encoding="utf-8")                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def run_bridge_with_pack(pack_path: Path, out_dir: Path):                 │
│      out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)                            │
│      exe = rebuild_bridge()                                                 │
│      env = os.environ.copy()                                               │
│      env["PLC_BRIDGE_PACK"] = str(pack_path)                               │
│      env["PLC_BRIDGE_OUT"] = str(out_dir)                                  │
│      run_checked([str(exe)], env=env)                                      │
│      return json.loads(...)                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: This is a minor quality gap. The instruction describes
PLC_BRIDGE_PACK as "optional," and the core algorithmic correctness is
fully verified through the file-overwrite approach. An agent that breaks
only this env var while correctly implementing all algorithms would still
be delivering a mostly correct solution.

================================================================================

The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

                          REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to reverse-engineer and repair a broken C++ PLC
bridge program that drives a line controller via RS-485, fixing bugs in wire
decoding, sequencer state transitions, timing gap validation, safety latch
logic, and ledger repair routines. The oracle solution rewrites five buggy
source files (op_a through op_e) to reconcile behavior with policy catalogs
and protocol captures, then rebuilds and runs the binary on multiple replay
packs. The test suite contains 13 functions that validate the output
compatibility report across bundled, disclosed, and secret fixture packs.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 15-19)
Problem: The Dockerfile installs pytest and pytest-json-ctrf into the image.
         These are test-only dependencies that should be installed in test.sh
         instead, since the agent does not need them to solve the task.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh \              │
│      && /root/.local/bin/uv venv /opt/tbench-verifier --python 3.13 \      │
│      && VIRTUAL_ENV=/opt/tbench-verifier /root/.local/bin/uv pip install \  │
│          pytest==8.4.1 \                                                    │
│          pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Move pytest installation to tests/test.sh and remove it from
the Dockerfile. The test runner should install its own dependencies:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # In test.sh, add before running pytest:                                   │
│  curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh                     │
│  source $HOME/.local/bin/env                                                │
│  uv venv .tbench-testing                                                    │
│  source .tbench-testing/bin/activate                                        │
│  uv pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Test dependencies in the image are generally discouraged because
they leak verifier infrastructure into the agent workspace. Since the agent
only needs build-essential/cmake/g++ to solve this task, pytest should live
exclusively in the test runner.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. test.sh Does Not Install Its Own Test Dependencies
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (lines 1-14)
Problem: test.sh relies on pytest being pre-installed in the Docker image
         (via /opt/tbench-verifier on PATH). If the image changes or the
         verifier venv is removed, tests will fail silently. The standard
         pattern is for test.sh to install its own dependencies.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  #!/bin/bash                                                                │
│                                                                             │
│  if [ "$PWD" = "/" ]; then                                                  │
│      echo "Error: No working directory set."                                │
│      exit 1                                                                 │
│  fi                                                                         │
│                                                                             │
│  pytest --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json /tests/test_outputs.py -rA          │
│                                                                             │
│  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then                                                      │
│    echo 1 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       │
│  else                                                                       │
│    echo 0 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       │
│  fi                                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Use the standard self-contained test.sh pattern that installs
uv and pytest before running tests.

Explanation: The standard test.sh format ensures tests are hermetic and do
not depend on image-level state that may drift.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction Could Clarify Bug Symptoms More Explicitly
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (line 1)

Current approach: The instruction describes high-level symptoms ("handshake
stalls", "transcript rejects", "gap faults", "estop transitions unsafe") but
does not pinpoint which source files are buggy or what the specific wrong
behaviors are.

Rationale: While ambiguity is intentional for a "hard" reverse-engineering
task, the dense single-paragraph format and terse phrasing could benefit from
at least separating requirements into bullet points for readability. The agent
must infer which of ~20+ source files contain the five bugs — appropriate for
the stated difficulty, but borderline on clarity.

output_data.review:
================================================================================
                          REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to reverse-engineer and repair a broken C++ PLC
bridge program that drives a line controller via RS-485, fixing bugs in wire
decoding, sequencer state transitions, timing gap validation, safety latch
logic, and ledger repair routines. The oracle solution rewrites five buggy
source files (op_a through op_e) to reconcile behavior with policy catalogs
and protocol captures, then rebuilds and runs the binary on multiple replay
packs. The test suite contains 13 functions that validate the output
compatibility report across bundled, disclosed, and secret fixture packs.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 15-19)
Problem: The Dockerfile installs pytest and pytest-json-ctrf into the image.
         These are test-only dependencies that should be installed in test.sh
         instead, since the agent does not need them to solve the task.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh \              │
│      && /root/.local/bin/uv venv /opt/tbench-verifier --python 3.13 \      │
│      && VIRTUAL_ENV=/opt/tbench-verifier /root/.local/bin/uv pip install \  │
│          pytest==8.4.1 \                                                    │
│          pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Move pytest installation to tests/test.sh and remove it from
the Dockerfile. The test runner should install its own dependencies:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # In test.sh, add before running pytest:                                   │
│  curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh                     │
│  source $HOME/.local/bin/env                                                │
│  uv venv .tbench-testing                                                    │
│  source .tbench-testing/bin/activate                                        │
│  uv pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Test dependencies in the image are generally discouraged because
they leak verifier infrastructure into the agent workspace. Since the agent
only needs build-essential/cmake/g++ to solve this task, pytest should live
exclusively in the test runner.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. test.sh Does Not Install Its Own Test Dependencies
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (lines 1-14)
Problem: test.sh relies on pytest being pre-installed in the Docker image
         (via /opt/tbench-verifier on PATH). If the image changes or the
         verifier venv is removed, tests will fail silently. The standard
         pattern is for test.sh to install its own dependencies.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  #!/bin/bash                                                                │
│                                                                             │
│  if [ "$PWD" = "/" ]; then                                                  │
│      echo "Error: No working directory set."                                │
│      exit 1                                                                 │
│  fi                                                                         │
│                                                                             │
│  pytest --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json /tests/test_outputs.py -rA          │
│                                                                             │
│  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then                                                      │
│    echo 1 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       │
│  else                                                                       │
│    echo 0 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       │
│  fi                                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Use the standard self-contained test.sh pattern that installs
uv and pytest before running tests.

Explanation: The standard test.sh format ensures tests are hermetic and do
not depend on image-level state that may drift.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction Could Clarify Bug Symptoms More Explicitly
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (line 1)

Current approach: The instruction describes high-level symptoms ("handshake
stalls", "transcript rejects", "gap faults", "estop transitions unsafe") but
does not pinpoint which source files are buggy or what the specific wrong
behaviors are.

Rationale: While ambiguity is intentional for a "hard" reverse-engineering
task, the dense single-paragraph format and terse phrasing could benefit from
at least separating requirements into bullet points for readability. The agent
must infer which of ~20+ source files contain the five bugs — appropriate for
the stated difficulty, but borderline on clarity.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

A well-constructed hard debugging/reverse-engineering task with a realistic
industrial C++ codebase. The anti-cheating design is strong: five bugs are
distributed across independent modules, hidden test fixtures prevent
hardcoding, and the agent must understand protocol semantics to fix them.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating via hidden fixture packs and generalization tests
  ✓ Realistic embedded-systems debugging scenario with proper C++ architecture
  ✓ Complete schema documentation and policy catalogs support solvability

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies (pytest) installed in Docker image instead of test.sh
  ✗ test.sh is non-standard (relies on image-provided pytest rather than
    self-installing)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, protocol reverse-engineering, embedded systems
           reasoning, codebase navigation

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move pytest installation from the Dockerfile into test.sh so the test
  runner is self-contained. The task logic and design are excellent otherwise.
================================================================================

Agent verifies behavior on fixture packs (noisy_gap, estop_skip) or runs ci/preflight.sh to confirm generalization across multiple replay packs, +2

Agent leaves safety/op_d.cpp as a no-op stub that always returns true, -5
Agent leaves ledger/op_e.cpp unchanged returning raw bytes without repair, -3

The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

                          REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to reverse-engineer and repair a broken C++ PLC
bridge program that drives a line controller via RS-485, fixing bugs in wire
decoding, sequencer state transitions, timing gap validation, safety latch
logic, and ledger repair routines. The oracle solution rewrites five buggy
source files (op_a through op_e) to reconcile behavior with policy catalogs
and protocol captures, then rebuilds and runs the binary on multiple replay
packs. The test suite contains 13 functions that validate the output
compatibility report across bundled, disclosed, and secret fixture packs.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 15-19)
Problem: The Dockerfile installs pytest and pytest-json-ctrf into the image.
         These are test-only dependencies that should be installed in test.sh
         instead, since the agent does not need them to solve the task.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh \              │
│      && /root/.local/bin/uv venv /opt/tbench-verifier --python 3.13 \      │
│      && VIRTUAL_ENV=/opt/tbench-verifier /root/.local/bin/uv pip install \  │
│          pytest==8.4.1 \                                                    │
│          pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Move pytest installation to tests/test.sh and remove it from
the Dockerfile. The test runner should install its own dependencies:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # In test.sh, add before running pytest:                                   │
│  curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh                     │
│  source $HOME/.local/bin/env                                                │
│  uv venv .tbench-testing                                                    │
│  source .tbench-testing/bin/activate                                        │
│  uv pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Test dependencies in the image are generally discouraged because
they leak verifier infrastructure into the agent workspace. Since the agent
only needs build-essential/cmake/g++ to solve this task, pytest should live
exclusively in the test runner.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. test.sh Does Not Install Its Own Test Dependencies
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (lines 1-14)
Problem: test.sh relies on pytest being pre-installed in the Docker image
         (via /opt/tbench-verifier on PATH). If the image changes or the
         verifier venv is removed, tests will fail silently. The standard
         pattern is for test.sh to install its own dependencies.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  #!/bin/bash                                                                │
│                                                                             │
│  if [ "$PWD" = "/" ]; then                                                  │
│      echo "Error: No working directory set."                                │
│      exit 1                                                                 │
│  fi                                                                         │
│                                                                             │
│  pytest --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json /tests/test_outputs.py -rA          │
│                                                                             │
│  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then                                                      │
│    echo 1 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       │
│  else                                                                       │
│    echo 0 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       │
│  fi                                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Use the standard self-contained test.sh pattern that installs
uv and pytest before running tests.

Explanation: The standard test.sh format ensures tests are hermetic and do
not depend on image-level state that may drift.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction Could Clarify Bug Symptoms More Explicitly
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (line 1)

Current approach: The instruction describes high-level symptoms ("handshake
stalls", "transcript rejects", "gap faults", "estop transitions unsafe") but
does not pinpoint which source files are buggy or what the specific wrong
behaviors are.

Rationale: While ambiguity is intentional for a "hard" reverse-engineering
task, the dense single-paragraph format and terse phrasing could benefit from
at least separating requirements into bullet points for readability. The agent
must infer which of ~20+ source files contain the five bugs — appropriate for
the stated difficulty, but borderline on clarity.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

A well-constructed hard debugging/reverse-engineering task with a realistic
industrial C++ codebase. The anti-cheating design is strong: five bugs are
distributed across independent modules, hidden test fixtures prevent
hardcoding, and the agent must understand protocol semantics to fix them.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating via hidden fixture packs and generalization tests
  ✓ Realistic embedded-systems debugging scenario with proper C++ architecture
  ✓ Complete schema documentation and policy catalogs support solvability

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies (pytest) installed in Docker image instead of test.sh
  ✗ test.sh is non-standard (relies on image-provided pytest rather than
    self-installing)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, protocol reverse-engineering, embedded systems
           reasoning, codebase navigation

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move pytest installation from the Dockerfile into test.sh so the test
  runner is self-contained. The task logic and design are excellent otherwise.

The test runner should install its own dependencies:\n\u250c\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2510\n\u2502  # In test.sh, add before running pytest:                                   \u2502\n\u2502  curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh                     \u2502\n\u2502  source $HOME/.local/bin/env                                                \u2502\n\u2502  uv venv .tbench-testing                                                    \u2502\n\u2502  source .tbench-testing/bin/activate                                        \u2502\n\u2502  uv pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                       \u2502\n\u2514\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2518\n\nExplanation: Test dependencies in the image are generally discouraged because\nthey leak verifier infrastructure into the agent workspace. Since the agent\nonly needs build-essential/cmake/g++ to solve this task, pytest should live\nexclusively in the test runner.\n\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n2. test.sh Does Not Install Its Own Test Dependencies\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nFile:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (lines 1-14)\nProblem: test.sh relies on pytest being pre-installed in the Docker image\n         (via /opt/tbench-verifier on PATH). -eq 0 ]; then                                                      \u2502\n\u2502    echo 1 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       \u2502\n\u2502  else                                                                       \u2502\n\u2502    echo 0 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       \u2502\n\u2502  fi                                                                         \u2502\n\u2514\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2518\n\nSuggested fix: Use the standard self-contained test.sh pattern that installs\nuv and pytest before running tests.\n\nExplanation: The standard test.sh format ensures tests are hermetic and do\nnot depend on image-level state that may drift.\n\n================================================================================\n                             SUGGESTIONS \ud83d\udca1\n================================================================================\n\n--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n1. The agent\nmust infer which of ~20+ source files contain the five bugs \u2014 appropriate for\nthe stated difficulty, but borderline on clarity.\n\n================================================================================\n                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT\n================================================================================\n\nA well-constructed hard debugging/reverse-engineering task with a realistic\nindustrial C++ codebase. The anti-cheating design is strong: five bugs are\ndistributed across independent modules, hidden test fixtures prevent\nhardcoding, and the agent must understand protocol semantics to fix them.\n\nKey Strengths:\n  \u2713 Excellent anti-cheating via hidden fixture packs and generalization tests\n  \u2713 Realistic embedded-systems debugging scenario with proper C++ architecture\n  \u2713 Complete schema documentation and policy catalogs support solvability\n\nKey Weaknesses:\n  \u2717 Test dependencies (pytest) installed in Docker image instead of test.sh\n  \u2717 test.sh is non-standard (relies on image-provided pytest rather than\n    self-installing)\n\nEvaluates: C++ debugging, protocol reverse-engineering, embedded systems\n           reasoning, codebase navigation\n\n================================================================================\n  RECOMMENDATION: \u26a0\ufe0f NEEDS REVISION\n\n  Move pytest installation from the Dockerfile into test.sh so the test\n  runner is self-contained. Difficulty: ✅ MEDIUM

Status: ✅ Solvable (all tests passed by at least one agent run)

Agent Performance:
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-6: 100.0% (5/5 runs)
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 60.0% (3/5 runs)

Reference Agents:
  • nop: 0.0% (0/1 runs)
  • oracle: 100.0% (3/3 runs)

Failure Breakdown:
  • nop: 1 other
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 2 other

Unit Tests Results:
  • test_p01: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p02: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p03: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p04: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p05: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p06: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p07: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p08: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p09: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p10: 8 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p11: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p12: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p13: 10 passed / 10 runs

Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ✅ PASS, ## Job Run Summary

### 1. Overall Results
- **2 trials total — 0 passed, 2 failed** (reward = 0.0 for both)
- Both trials used the same task (C++ PLC bridge repair) but appear to be different agent runs (`tbench-task__3hfRZ4a`, `tbench-task__hMeDbU5`)
- The grading system uses **binary all-or-nothing scoring** — partial progress earns no reward

### 2. Common Failure Patterns

Both trials failed on **test_p10 (estop_skip fixture)**, making it the universal blocker:

| Failure | 3hfRZ4a | hMeDbU5 |
|---|---|---|
| test_p10 (estop_skip) | ✗ — over-generalized `is_move_class` to include `kOpEstop`, applied wrong timing threshold | ✗ — decoupled ESTOP from sequence state machine entirely, breaking `sequence_ok` for MOVE-after-ESTOP |
| test_p12 (hidden fixture) | ✗ — ledger repair didn't generalize; returned `frames_recovered=0`, `link_band=noisy` | ✓ (passed) |

**Root cause shared by both**: Both agents misunderstood the relationship between `kOpEstop` (opcode 4) and the arm-history state machine. The distinction between `kOpMove` (3) and `kOpEstop` (4) in `include/wire/codec.hpp` was a critical signal both agents missed or mishandled.

### 3. Hack Check
**No cheating detected.** Both trials passed `reward_hacking` checks cleanly:
- Neither agent modified test files, accessed `solution/`, or wrote to `/logs/verifier/reward.txt`
- All edits were legitimate C++ source repairs under `/app`

### 4. Systematic Instruction Issues
**None identified** — both trials passed `task_specification`. The spec adequately distinguishes:
- `sequence_ok`: arm history state machine (ESTOP → must RESET before MOVE)
- `safe_mode`: estop-path safety check

Both failures were **agent reasoning errors**, not spec gaps. That said, the estop/move-class boundary is a subtle distinction that tripped up both agents independently — consider whether the spec could more explicitly flag that `kOpEstop` is *not* a move-class opcode and must remain in the state machine.

### 5. Progress for Failed Trials
Both agents made **substantial progress** before falling short:

| Metric | 3hfRZ4a | hMeDbU5 |
|---|---|---|
| Tests passed | 11 / 13 | 12 / 13 |
| Core fields correct | ✓ `link_band=tight`, `timing_ok=true`, `restore_kind=live`, `frames_recovered=1` | ✓ All fields correct in bundled session |
| Files repaired | `timing/op_c.cpp`, `config/load.cpp`, `sim/ledger_fold.cpp` | `timing/op_c.cpp`, `ledger/op_e.cpp`, `sim/timing_fold.cpp`, `safety/op_d.cpp`, `config/load.cpp` |

Average completion: **~88% of tests passing**, with correct core output values in both cases. The binary scoring makes the 0.0 reward misleading — both agents demonstrated strong partial task mastery.

### 6. Key Differences Between Agents
`hMeDbU5` outperformed `3hfRZ4a` on depth of repair:
- **hMeDbU5** fixed more files (5 vs. 3), implemented actual frame recovery via `wire::op_enc`, and fixed the handshake check — passing test_p12 where the other agent failed
- **3hfRZ4a** failed an additional test (test_p12) due to incomplete ledger repair generalization
- Both converged on the same estop misclassification error for test_p10, just via different mechanisms

**Recommendation**: The estop/move-class boundary deserves a targeted fix in the task prompt or a hint in the schema doc. One shared failure pattern causing a 0% pass rate across all trials suggests this is a solvable reliability gap.

✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: All behaviors mentioned in the instruction are exercised: the bundled session pack is run and all 8 schema fields are checked; both public fixtures (noisy_gap.json, estop_skip.json) are replayed via PLC_BRIDGE_PACK; two undisclosed grading fixtures stress-test generalization; a test confirms the session pack file is not mutated by env-var replays; and a test confirms the report exposes only the 8 public fields. The preflight.sh smoke runs are implicitly covered by the same fixture replay tests.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a descriptive name and a one-line docstring stating exactly what is being checked (e.g. 'Bundled pack reports tight link band after repair', 'Undisclosed pack with two ledger repairs via PLC_BRIDGE_PACK'). The shared `assert_report` helper keeps fixture assertions compact and readable. Helper functions `rebuild_bridge`, `run_bridge`, and `lab_schema_tag` are cleanly separated. The file is easy to maintain and extend.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: Internet is disabled (`allow_internet = false`). The Dockerfile copies only `environment/` subdirectories — tests/ and solution/ never enter the image. The two hidden grading fixtures live at `/tests/secret_fixture.json` and `/tests/secret_fixture_2.json`, which are mounted only at verify time and are invisible to the agent at build/run time. The instruction explicitly forbids pack_id shortcuts ('Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts'). The test dynamically reads the schema tag from the source file rather than comparing against a hardcoded string, preventing trivial JSON fabrication. The verifier always rebuilds the binary from source, preventing pre-built binary substitution.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` file (present in the environment) provides a normative table listing all 8 fields with name, type, and precise semantics. The instruction.md explicitly references this file and lists all field names. The output file path is stated as `/app/output/compatibility_report.json`. The schema is complete and definitional, not merely illustrative.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The base image is pinned by digest (`debian:bookworm-slim@sha256:4724b8...`). All apt packages specify exact Debian version strings (e.g., `cmake=3.25.1-1`, `g++=4:12.2.0-3`). The uv installer URL pins version 0.9.5. Both Python packages are pinned (`pytest==8.4.1`, `pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5`).
✅ pass - typos: All file paths in instruction.md, the Dockerfile, tests, and source files are consistent and correct: `/app/data/captures/`, `/app/data/firmware/`, `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`, `/app/catalog/`, `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md`, `/app/data/fixtures/`, `/app/ci/preflight.sh`. Env vars PLC_BRIDGE_PACK and PLC_BRIDGE_OUT match in main.cpp and tests. Hidden fixture paths in the tests match the actual files in tests/. No typos found.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile contains only COPY instructions for the subdirectories under `environment/` (CMakeLists.txt, cmd, wire, seq, timing, safety, ledger, sim, metrics, config, util, decoy, data, catalog, deploy, docs, ci, bench, include). Neither `tests/` nor `solution/` is copied into the image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh generates complete, semantically correct C++ implementations for five functions: op_a (rev-aware wire decoding), op_b (state machine with proper estop guard), op_c (timing gate reading catalog policy via config::gap_after_reset_ms / gap_arm_to_move_ms), op_d (safety latch check requiring reset before post-estop arm/move), and op_e (ledger repair via wire::op_enc). It then runs cmake, builds, installs, and executes the binary. The answer is derived by compiling and running real simulation logic, not by printing predetermined strings.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: The instruction explicitly states the agent must 'leave /app/output/compatibility_report.json matching that run.' The exact filename `compatibility_report.json` and its directory `/app/output/` are both stated. The tests look for exactly this path (`REPORT = OUT / 'compatibility_report.json'` where `OUT = APP / 'output'`).

Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite's combination of source recompilation and undisclosed fixtures
makes shortcutting the core algorithms infeasible for the ledger and timing
fixes; the one minor gap in safety-check edge coverage does not materially
weaken the suite.

Strengths:  The verifier rebuilds the binary from source (preventing static
output file tricks) and runs two hidden fixtures the agent cannot access,
requiring correct algorithmic generalisation across ledger repair, timing
thresholds, and queue-skew detection.

Weaknesses: The safety/estop check is only tested in one direction (estop
present without recovery → False); no test exercises the case where an estop
IS properly followed by a reset before the next move.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 14 tests covering all eight output fields across five
fixture packs (bundled, noisy_gap, estop_skip, and two hidden). By
recompiling the C++ source at verify time and running the resulting binary
against undisclosed input packs, the suite effectively prevents hardcoded
shortcuts for ledger repair and timing reconciliation. The only gap is that
the safety check (op_d) can be implemented with a simplified heuristic
("return false if any estop opcode exists") that passes every test yet
violates the instruction's stated semantics of requiring a reset before
move-class ops resume after estop.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Safety check missing estop-recovery positive case
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:152-179 (fixture tests)
Problem: No fixture contains a transcript where an estop is followed by a
proper reset before a subsequent move, then expects safe_mode=True. All
fixtures with estop (estop_skip.json) have no intervening reset, and all
hidden fixtures lack estop entirely. An agent could implement op_d as
"return false if estop opcode is present" rather than the correct stateful
check "estop requires reset before next move."

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # estop_skip: transcript [5,2,4,3] → safe_mode=False                      │
│  # hidden fixtures: transcripts without estop → safe_mode=True              │
│  # No fixture with pattern [5,2,4,5,2,3] → safe_mode=True                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Add a hidden fixture with transcript [5,2,4,5,2,3] (estop recovered)    │
│  # and assert safe_mode=True, proving op_d checks reset-after-estop logic  │
│  # rather than simply detecting estop presence.                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The instruction states "Estop paths include reset before
move-class ops resume," implying a stateful check. A naive "if estop in
transcript → false" passes all tests but violates the spec for inputs
where estop IS safely recovered. This is low-impact because the agent
still must implement non-trivial logic and the hidden fixtures guard the
other three repair targets.

================================================================================

The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

# Rubric 1
Agent fixes wire/op_a.cpp to handle RevB as a 5-byte format where the first 4 bytes store the full 32-bit LE value and byte 5 is a revision tag, +3
Agent fixes wire/op_enc.cpp to emit 5 bytes for RevB (4-byte LE value followed by revision tag byte 0x02) matching the canonical on-wire format, +3
Agent validates that the 5th byte (raw[4]) matches the revision parameter in op_a's RevB decode path to reject malformed frames, +2
Agent incorrectly treats RevB values as 24-bit (3-byte) quantities instead of full 32-bit values with an appended 5th tag byte, -2

# Rubric 2
Agent fixes seq/op_b.cpp to reject kOpArm when the current state_x is kStateStopped, preventing arming from the stopped state, +3
Agent preserves correct handling of other transitions: reset moves to idle, move requires armed state, poll always succeeds, estop moves to stopped, +2
Agent corrupts the state machine by removing valid transitions or adding incorrect state changes, -3

# Rubric 3
Agent modifies timing/op_c.cpp to read gap thresholds from /app/catalog/gap_policy.toml via config loader functions instead of using hardcoded values, +3
Agent correctly maps reset-to-arm transition to gap_after_reset_ms (40ms) and arm-to-move transition to gap_arm_to_move_ms (30ms) rather than the original wrong pair (arm-to-poll), +3
Agent uses poll_min_gap_ms (10ms) from catalog for the poll transition minimum gap check, +1
Agent leaves hardcoded timing thresholds that do not match the values specified in catalog/gap_policy.toml, -2

# Rubric 4
Agent implements estop latching logic in safety/op_d.cpp requiring a kOpReset after any kOpEstop before move-class operations can resume, +3
Agent correctly returns false when kOpMove is attempted after kOpEstop without an intervening kOpReset, detecting the unsafe transition, +3
Agent also blocks kOpArm after estop without reset, matching the catalog require_reset_after_estop policy for both move and arm operations, +2
Agent leaves the safety function as a stub that unconditionally returns true without implementing actual estop enforcement, -5

# Rubric 5

Agent successfully builds the project with cmake and installs the binary to /app/bin/plc_bridge, +2
Agent produces /app/output/compatibility_report.json with restore_kind set to live indicating corrected service, +3

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires repairing a broken C++ PLC bridge that simulates an
industrial RS-485 line controller. The agent must diagnose and fix five broken
modules (wire decoding, sequence tracking, timing gate, safety latch, ledger
repair) by cross-referencing hex captures, policy catalogs, and replay packs,
then rebuild and produce a correct compatibility report. The test suite uses
13 functions including hidden fixtures to verify generalization, preventing
hardcoded shortcuts.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Pre-Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 16-20)
Problem: pytest==8.4.1 and pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5 are installed into a
         virtualenv in the Docker image. The test.sh relies on this rather
         than installing its own dependencies, reducing portability to other
         harnesses.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh \               │
│      && /root/.local/bin/uv venv /opt/tbench-verifier --python 3.13 \       │
│      && VIRTUAL_ENV=/opt/tbench-verifier /root/.local/bin/uv pip install \   │
│          pytest==8.4.1 \                                                    │
│          pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Move pytest installation into test.sh using the standard
pattern (install uv, create venv, pip install pytest, then run). Remove
the pytest-related lines and /opt/tbench-verifier from the Dockerfile.

Explanation: Test-only dependencies should be installed in test.sh so the
image stays focused on the agent's task environment. Pytest is not useful
for the agent's C++ debugging work, so the "agent could use it" exception
does not apply.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted debugging task that simulates a realistic
industrial embedded scenario. The five deliberately broken modules each test a
distinct skill, hidden test fixtures prevent shortcutting, and the overall
architecture is cohesive and fair.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating design via hidden fixtures and full binary rebuild
  ✓ Complete behavior coverage — all schema fields tested across multiple packs
  ✓ Realistic difficulty with clear signal from environment (catalog, docs, hex)

✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: Internet is disabled (`allow_internet = false`). The Dockerfile copies only `environment/` subdirectories — tests/ and solution/ never enter the image. The instruction explicitly forbids pack_id shortcuts ('Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts'). The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite's combination of source recompilation and undisclosed fixtures
makes shortcutting the core algorithms infeasible for the ledger and timing
fixes; the one minor gap in safety-check edge coverage does not materially
weaken the suite.

Strengths:  The verifier rebuilds the binary from source (preventing static
output file tricks) and runs two hidden fixtures the agent cannot access,
requiring correct algorithmic generalisation across ledger repair, timing
thresholds, and queue-skew detection.

Weaknesses: The safety/estop check is only tested in one direction (estop
present without recovery → False); no test exercises the case where an estop
IS properly followed by a reset before the next move.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 14 tests covering all eight output fields across five
fixture packs (bundled, noisy_gap, estop_skip, and two hidden). By
recompiling the C++ source at verify time and running the resulting binary
against undisclosed input packs, the suite effectively prevents hardcoded
shortcuts for ledger repair and timing reconciliation. The only gap is that
the safety check (op_d) can be implemented with a simplified heuristic
("return false if any estop opcode exists") that passes every test yet
violates the instruction's stated semantics of requiring a reset before
move-class ops resume after estop.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Safety check missing estop-recovery positive case
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:152-179 (fixture tests)
Problem: No fixture contains a transcript where an estop is followed by a
proper reset before a subsequent move, then expects safe_mode=True. All
fixtures with estop (estop_skip.json) have no intervening reset, and all
hidden fixtures lack estop entirely. An agent could implement op_d as
"return false if estop opcode is present" rather than the correct stateful
check "estop requires reset before next move."

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # estop_skip: transcript [5,2,4,3] → safe_mode=False                      │
│  # hidden fixtures: transcripts without estop → safe_mode=True              │
│  # No fixture with pattern [5,2,4,5,2,3] → safe_mode=True                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Add a hidden fixture with transcript [5,2,4,5,2,3] (estop recovered)    │
│  # and assert safe_mode=True, proving op_d checks reset-after-estop logic  │
│  # rather than simply detecting estop presence.                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The instruction states "Estop paths include reset before
move-class ops resume," implying a stateful check. A naive "if estop in
transcript → false" passes all tests but violates the spec for inputs
where estop IS safely recovered. This is low-impact because the agent
still must implement non-trivial logic and the hidden fixtures guard the
other three repair targets.

================================================================================
