Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

# Rubric 1
Agent fixes wire/op_a.cpp to handle RevB as a 5-byte format where the first 4 bytes store the full 32-bit LE value and byte 5 is a revision tag, +3
Agent fixes wire/op_enc.cpp to emit 5 bytes for RevB (4-byte LE value followed by revision tag byte 0x02) matching the canonical on-wire format, +3
Agent validates that the 5th byte (raw[4]) matches the revision parameter in op_a's RevB decode path to reject malformed frames, +2
Agent incorrectly treats RevB values as 24-bit (3-byte) quantities instead of full 32-bit values with an appended 5th tag byte, -2

# Rubric 2
Agent fixes seq/op_b.cpp to reject kOpArm when the current state_x is kStateStopped, preventing arming from the stopped state, +3
Agent preserves correct handling of other transitions: reset moves to idle, move requires armed state, poll always succeeds, estop moves to stopped, +2
Agent corrupts the state machine by removing valid transitions or adding incorrect state changes, -3

# Rubric 3
Agent modifies timing/op_c.cpp to read gap thresholds from /app/catalog/gap_policy.toml via config loader functions instead of using hardcoded values, +3
Agent correctly maps reset-to-arm transition to gap_after_reset_ms (40ms) and arm-to-move transition to gap_arm_to_move_ms (30ms) rather than the original wrong pair (arm-to-poll), +3
Agent uses poll_min_gap_ms (10ms) from catalog for the poll transition minimum gap check, +1
Agent leaves hardcoded timing thresholds that do not match the values specified in catalog/gap_policy.toml, -2

# Rubric 4
Agent implements estop latching logic in safety/op_d.cpp requiring a kOpReset after any kOpEstop before move-class operations can resume, +3
Agent correctly returns false when kOpMove is attempted after kOpEstop without an intervening kOpReset, detecting the unsafe transition, +3
Agent also blocks kOpArm after estop without reset, matching the catalog require_reset_after_estop policy for both move and arm operations, +2
Agent leaves the safety function as a stub that unconditionally returns true without implementing actual estop enforcement, -5

# Rubric 5
Agent fixes ledger/op_e.cpp to re-encode rows using wire::op_enc(rev, value) instead of returning raw bytes unchanged, enabling frame recovery, +3
Agent successfully builds the project with cmake and installs the binary to /app/bin/plc_bridge, +2
Agent produces /app/output/compatibility_report.json with restore_kind set to live indicating corrected service, +3
Agent runs the binary against fixture packs under /app/data/fixtures/ in addition to the bundled session pack to validate generalization, +2
Agent hardcodes report field values in main.cpp or metrics/report.cpp rather than fixing the underlying simulation logic, -5

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires repairing a broken C++ PLC bridge that simulates an
industrial RS-485 line controller. The agent must diagnose and fix five broken
modules (wire decoding, sequence tracking, timing gate, safety latch, ledger
repair) by cross-referencing hex captures, policy catalogs, and replay packs,
then rebuild and produce a correct compatibility report. The test suite uses
13 functions including hidden fixtures to verify generalization, preventing
hardcoded shortcuts.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Pre-Installed in Dockerfile

Problem: pytest==8.4.1 and pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5 are installed into a
         virtualenv in the Docker image. The test.sh relies on this rather
         than installing its own dependencies, reducing portability to other
         harnesses.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh \               │
│      && /root/.local/bin/uv venv /opt/tbench-verifier --python 3.13 \       │
│      && VIRTUAL_ENV=/opt/tbench-verifier /root/.local/bin/uv pip install \   │
│          pytest==8.4.1 \                                                    │
│          pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Move pytest installation into test.sh using the standard
pattern (install uv, create venv, pip install pytest, then run). Remove
the pytest-related lines and /opt/tbench-verifier from the Dockerfile.

Explanation: Test-only dependencies should be installed in test.sh so the
image stays focused on the agent's task environment. Pytest is not useful
for the agent's C++ debugging work, so the "agent could use it" exception
does not apply.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted debugging task that simulates a realistic
industrial embedded scenario. The five deliberately broken modules each test a
distinct skill, hidden test fixtures prevent shortcutting, and the overall
architecture is cohesive and fair.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating design via hidden fixtures and full binary rebuild
  ✓ Complete behavior coverage — all schema fields tested across multiple packs
  ✓ Realistic difficulty with clear signal from environment (catalog, docs, hex)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies bundled in the image rather than in test.sh

Evaluates: C++ debugging, protocol reverse-engineering, embedded safety logic,
           build-system literacy

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move pytest installation from the Dockerfile into test.sh to follow the
  standard harness-portable pattern. No other blocking issues.

The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

================================================================================
# Rubric 1
Agent fixes wire/op_a.cpp to handle RevB as a 5-byte format where the first 4 bytes store the full 32-bit LE value and byte 5 is a revision tag, +3
Agent fixes wire/op_enc.cpp to emit 5 bytes for RevB (4-byte LE value followed by revision tag byte 0x02) matching the canonical on-wire format, +3
Agent validates that the 5th byte (raw[4]) matches the revision parameter in op_a's RevB decode path to reject malformed frames, +2
Agent incorrectly treats RevB values as 24-bit (3-byte) quantities instead of full 32-bit values with an appended 5th tag byte, -2

# Rubric 2
Agent fixes seq/op_b.cpp to reject kOpArm when the current state_x is kStateStopped, preventing arming from the stopped state, +3
Agent preserves correct handling of other transitions: reset moves to idle, move requires armed state, poll always succeeds, estop moves to stopped, +2
Agent corrupts the state machine by removing valid transitions or adding incorrect state changes, -3

# Rubric 3
Agent modifies timing/op_c.cpp to read gap thresholds from /app/catalog/gap_policy.toml via config loader functions instead of using hardcoded values, +3
Agent correctly maps reset-to-arm transition to gap_after_reset_ms (40ms) and arm-to-move transition to gap_arm_to_move_ms (30ms) rather than the original wrong pair (arm-to-poll), +3
Agent uses poll_min_gap_ms (10ms) from catalog for the poll transition minimum gap check, +1
Agent leaves hardcoded timing thresholds that do not match the values specified in catalog/gap_policy.toml, -2

# Rubric 4
Agent implements estop latching logic in safety/op_d.cpp requiring a kOpReset after any kOpEstop before move-class operations can resume, +3
Agent correctly returns false when kOpMove is attempted after kOpEstop without an intervening kOpReset, detecting the unsafe transition, +3
Agent also blocks kOpArm after estop without reset, matching the catalog require_reset_after_estop policy for both move and arm operations, +2
Agent leaves the safety function as a stub that unconditionally returns true without implementing actual estop enforcement, -5

# Rubric 5
Agent fixes ledger/op_e.cpp to re-encode rows using wire::op_enc(rev, value) instead of returning raw bytes unchanged, enabling frame recovery, +3
Agent successfully builds the project with cmake and installs the binary to /app/bin/plc_bridge, +2
Agent produces /app/output/compatibility_report.json with restore_kind set to live indicating corrected service, +3
Agent runs the binary against fixture packs under /app/data/fixtures/ in addition to the bundled session pack to validate generalization, +2
Agent hardcodes report field values in main.cpp or metrics/report.cpp rather than fixing the underlying simulation logic, -5

                          REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires repairing a broken C++ PLC bridge that simulates an
industrial RS-485 line controller. The agent must diagnose and fix five broken
modules (wire decoding, sequence tracking, timing gate, safety latch, ledger
repair) by cross-referencing hex captures, policy catalogs, and replay packs,
then rebuild and produce a correct compatibility report. The test suite uses
13 functions including hidden fixtures to verify generalization, preventing
hardcoded shortcuts.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Pre-Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 16-20)
Problem: pytest==8.4.1 and pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5 are installed into a
         virtualenv in the Docker image. The test.sh relies on this rather
         than installing its own dependencies, reducing portability to other
         harnesses.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh \               │
│      && /root/.local/bin/uv venv /opt/tbench-verifier --python 3.13 \       │
│      && VIRTUAL_ENV=/opt/tbench-verifier /root/.local/bin/uv pip install \   │
│          pytest==8.4.1 \                                                    │
│          pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Move pytest installation into test.sh using the standard
pattern (install uv, create venv, pip install pytest, then run). Remove
the pytest-related lines and /opt/tbench-verifier from the Dockerfile.

Explanation: Test-only dependencies should be installed in test.sh so the
image stays focused on the agent's task environment. Pytest is not useful
for the agent's C++ debugging work, so the "agent could use it" exception
does not apply.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted debugging task that simulates a realistic
industrial embedded scenario. The five deliberately broken modules each test a
distinct skill, hidden test fixtures prevent shortcutting, and the overall
architecture is cohesive and fair.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating design via hidden fixtures and full binary rebuild
  ✓ Complete behavior coverage — all schema fields tested across multiple packs
  ✓ Realistic difficulty with clear signal from environment (catalog, docs, hex)

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies bundled in the image rather than in test.sh

Evaluates: C++ debugging, protocol reverse-engineering, embedded safety logic,
           build-system literacy

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move pytest installation from the Dockerfile into test.sh to follow the
  standard harness-portable pattern. No other blocking issues.

Difficulty: ✅ MEDIUM

Status: ✅ Solvable (all tests passed by at least one agent run)

Agent Performance:
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-6: 100.0% (5/5 runs)
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 40.0% (2/5 runs)

Reference Agents:
  • nop: 0.0% (0/1 runs)
  • oracle: 100.0% (3/3 runs)

Failure Breakdown:
  • nop: 1 other
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 3 other

Unit Tests Results:
  • test_bundled_report_schema_tag_matches_lab_marker: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_bundled_pack_reports_tight_link_band: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_bundled_pack_completes_reset_to_arm_handshake: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_bundled_pack_recovers_single_corrupt_ledger_row: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_bundled_transcript_respects_arm_history: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_bundled_transcript_gaps_satisfy_policy: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_bundled_pack_reports_safe_estop_handling: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_bundled_pack_closes_with_live_restore: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_plc_bridge_pack_env_replays_noisy_gap_fixture: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_plc_bridge_pack_env_replays_estop_skip_fixture: 8 passed / 10 runs
  • test_compatibility_report_exposes_only_public_fields: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_plc_bridge_pack_env_replays_hidden_two_frame_repair_pack: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_plc_bridge_pack_env_replays_hidden_queue_skew_pack: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_default_session_pack_unchanged_after_env_fixture_runs: 10 passed / 10 runs

Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ✅ PASS, ## Job Summary

### 1. Overall Results
- **3 trials, 0 passed** (all-or-nothing scoring; reward = 0.0 for all)
- All agents achieved **13/14 tests passing** — remarkably consistent near-misses
- No model/agent differentiation is visible in the data (single agent type across all trials)

---

### 2. Common Failure Patterns

Two distinct failure modes emerged, both involving misinterpretation of `sequence_ok` vs. `safe_mode`:

**Pattern A — Wrong `seq/op_b` edit (2/3 trials):**
`tbench-task__zpJAKwU` and `tbench-task__LFYegXT` both unnecessarily modified `seq/op_b.cpp`, reasoning that Estop→Move rejection belonged only in `safe_mode` and not `sequence_ok`. This broke `test_plc_bridge_pack_env_replays_estop_skip_fixture` in both cases. The original `seq/op_b.cpp` behavior was *already correct* — agents introduced a regression fixing something that wasn't broken.

**Pattern B — Hidden fixture generalization failure (1/3 trials):**
`tbench-task__vDECLeo` avoided the `seq/op_b` mistake but failed `test_plc_bridge_pack_env_replays_hidden_two_frame_repair_pack`. Its ledger repair logic correctly handled single-row repair (`frames_recovered=1`) but returned 0 for the hidden two-row scenario (`frames_recovered=2`).

**The core bugs fixed correctly by all agents** (showing consistent diagnosis capability):
- Hardcoded timing thresholds in `timing/op_c.cpp`
- Stub `ledger/op_e.cpp`
- Stub `safety/op_d.cpp`
- `ledger_fold.cpp` repair counting logic
- `main.cpp` ignoring the positional pack argument

---

### 3. Hack Check
**No cheating detected.** All three trials passed `reward_hacking` checks. Agents worked exclusively through legitimate source edits, rebuilds, and binary execution. No writes to `reward.txt`/`reward.json`, no reads from `solution/` directories, no test file modifications.

---

### 4. Systematic Instruction Issues

The `sequence_ok` / `safe_mode` boundary is a recurring confusion point (2/3 failures), suggesting a **spec clarity gap** worth addressing — even though both `task_specification` checks technically passed. The schema descriptions are:
- `sequence_ok`: *"Transcript opcodes respect arm history rules"*
- `safe_mode`: *"Estop paths include reset before move-class ops resume"*

These are semantically overlapping to a reader who doesn't know the implementation. Agents consistently concluded that Estop→Move rejection was *only* a `safe_mode` concern, not an arm history rule. Adding an explicit note like *"estop resets the armed state; a Reset opcode is required before Move resumes — this is an arm history rule enforced by `sequence_ok`"* would likely prevent this class of failure.

---

### 5. Progress on Failed Trials
All agents reached **13/14 (93%)** — they are one targeted fix away from a passing score. The failures are:
- `tbench-task__zpJAKwU` / `tbench-task__LFYegXT`: Revert the `seq/op_b.cpp` change
- `tbench-task__vDECLeo`: Fix ledger repair loop to accumulate all shadow-row mismatches, not just the first

---

### 6. Key Differences Between Trials

| Trial | Failing Test | Root Cause | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| `vDECLeo` | `hidden_two_frame_repair` | Repair logic didn't generalize to N>1 rows | Logic gap |
| `zpJAKwU` | `estop_skip_fixture` | Unnecessary `seq/op_b` regression | Incorrect edit |
| `LFYegXT` | `estop_skip_fixture` | Same unnecessary `seq/op_b` regression | Incorrect edit |

The 2/3 identical failure mode on `seq/op_b` is the dominant problem. The spec clarification above would likely convert those two trials to passes immediately.

[Container] 2026/05/31 10:49:42.498439 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

2026-05-31 11:00:06,067 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (43 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite uses source rebuild, hidden fixtures, and diverse scenarios
that together prevent meaningful shortcuts while verifying all core algorithms.

Strengths:  Tests rebuild the binary from agent-modified source at verification
time, and two undisclosed fixture packs (inaccessible to the agent) test
generalization of every output field with exact value assertions.

Weaknesses: The safety check (op_d) is only tested for the negative case
(estop without recovery → false) and no-estop (→ true); the positive case
(estop properly followed by reset before move → true) is never exercised.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 13 pytest assertions covering all 8 report fields
across 5 input packs (3 visible, 2 hidden). The verifier rebuilds the C++
binary from source, preventing pre-compiled binary injection. Hidden
fixtures with unique field combinations (e.g., frames_recovered=2,
link_band="noisy" from queue skew) force correct algorithmic implementations
for ledger repair, timing gate, and safety checks. The diverse gap values
across packs (12ms fails, 15ms passes for arm→poll) constrain thresholds
enough that naive single-value shortcuts fail against the hidden fixtures.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Safety op_d only tested for negative/absent estop — no recovery path
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:152-216 (all fixture assertions for safe_mode)
Problem: The safety function (op_d) is tested only in two scenarios: (1) no
estop in transcript → safe_mode=true, and (2) estop followed by move without
reset → safe_mode=false. No fixture tests the case where estop IS properly
handled (estop → reset → arm → move → safe_mode=true). A naive
implementation that returns false whenever any estop opcode is present would
pass all tests.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # estop_skip: expects safe_mode=False (estop then move, no reset)         │
│  # bundled/noisy/secret1/secret2: expects safe_mode=True (no estop)        │
│  # No fixture has estop + proper reset + move → True                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Add a fixture with transcript like:                                     │
│  # [reset, arm, estop, reset, arm, move] → safe_mode=True                  │
│  # This forces the agent to track state properly rather than               │
│  # using "estop present → false" shortcut                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An agent could implement op_d as simply checking whether opcode 4
(estop) appears anywhere in the transcript and returning false if so. This
passes all current tests because no fixture combines estop presence with a
safe_mode=true expectation. The shortcut is only marginally simpler than the
correct state-tracking implementation, and the agent still needs to identify
estop as the relevant concept, so exploitation risk is low.

================================================================================

Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

Review output: ================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to debug and repair a C++ PLC bridge that drives
an industrial line controller via RS-485 protocol. The broken sources contain
five distinct bugs: wire codec ignoring revision bytes, missing state-transition
guards, hardcoded timing thresholds instead of policy lookups, a no-op safety
module, and a no-op ledger repair function. The agent must reverse-engineer
correct behavior from captures, catalog policy files, and replay packs, then
fix the C++ sources so the rebuilt binary produces correct compatibility
reports for both disclosed and undisclosed fixture packs. The test suite
verifies all 8 report fields across the bundled session pack, two known
fixtures, and two hidden grading fixtures—rebuilding from the agent's source
code each time to prevent shortcut answers.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Oracle Solution Missing Directory Creation for /app/bin
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/solution/solve.sh (line 139)
Problem: The solution runs `install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/
         plc_bridge` but the Dockerfile never creates the /app/bin/ directory.
         GNU `install` without the `-D` flag does not create parent
         directories, so the oracle would fail when run standalone.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/plc_bridge                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  mkdir -p /app/bin                                                          │
│  install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/plc_bridge                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The test suite independently creates the directory via Python's
`Path.mkdir(parents=True)` in `rebuild_bridge()`, so grading still works. But
the oracle solution should also work when executed directly to validate task
solvability. The same issue exists in `environment/ci/preflight.sh`.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Use a More Descriptive Task Directory Name
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/ (directory name)

Current approach: The task directory is named "tbench-task" which is generic
and does not convey what the task is about.

Suggested improvement: Use a domain-descriptive kebab-case name such as
`plc-bridge-rs485-repair` or `legacy-plc-wire-compat`.

Rationale: A descriptive directory name helps with task discovery, cataloging,
and distinguishing this task from others in a task suite.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an exceptionally well-designed hard debugging task that requires deep
C++ comprehension, protocol reverse-engineering, and state-machine reasoning
across five independent subsystems. The anti-cheating measures are strong:
tests rebuild from source and grade against hidden fixtures.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Five distinct, interrelated bugs requiring independent diagnosis — each
    tied to a different protocol subsystem (codec, sequencer, timing, safety,
    ledger)
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: tests always rebuild from agent-modified source and
    include undisclosed grading fixtures that test generalization
  ✓ Comprehensive 14-test suite with excellent docstrings, full schema
    validation, and cross-fixture integrity checks

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Oracle solution would fail standalone due to missing /app/bin directory
    creation (tests unaffected)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, protocol reverse-engineering, state-machine analysis,
           reading policy/config files to infer correct behavior

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Add `mkdir -p /app/bin` before the install command in both solve.sh and
  ci/preflight.sh. Once fixed, the task is ready to use — all other aspects
  (structure, tests, Dockerfile, anti-cheating) are excellent.
================================================================================

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction explicitly names the output file (/app/output/compatibility_report.json), the binary to build (/app/bin/plc_bridge), the session pack to run (/app/data/session_pack.json), the env vars PLC_BRIDGE_PACK and PLC_BRIDGE_OUT, the schema reference doc (/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md listing all 8 fields with types and meanings), the fixtures directory to replay, and the 'no pack_id shortcuts' constraint tested by the hidden packs. The instruction frames the task as a debugging/repair exercise where the agent must reconcile the simulation against catalog policy — the correct field values emerge from correct implementation, not explicit enumeration, which is appropriate for this task category. Two minor implicit behaviors (exactly 8 output keys; session pack file must remain byte-identical after env-override runs) are tested but not spelled out verbatim; however both are direct corollaries of the schema definition and read-only env-override semantics, making them only lightly implied rather than undisclosed.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: Every behavior described in the instruction is covered by a test: building the binary, running on the bundled session pack, verifying the output file at the specified path, exercising PLC_BRIDGE_PACK and PLC_BRIDGE_OUT env vars, replaying each public fixture (noisy_gap, estop_skip), and generalizing to undisclosed packs (secret_fixture.json and secret_fixture_2.json with distinct expected values). The preflight.sh script is not directly tested but it's documented as a smoke-test helper, not a grading artifact.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: test_outputs.py is clearly organized: constants at the top, helper functions (run_bridge, rebuild_bridge, lab_schema_tag, assert_report) that abstract common setup, a module-scoped fixture for the bundled report, and individual test functions with descriptive names and one-line docstrings. Tests follow a consistent progression: bundled-pack field checks → fixture env-var checks → schema-key count → hidden-fixture checks → immutability check. The structure is readable and maintainable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The Dockerfile copies only the environment/ subtree into /app; the tests/ and solution/ directories are never present in the agent's image. The two secret grading fixtures (/tests/secret_fixture.json and /tests/secret_fixture_2.json) are injected only at verify time, so the agent cannot read them or their expected field values. The instruction explicitly prohibits pack_id shortcuts, and the hidden fixture values (frames_recovered=2 and link_band='noisy') differ from the bundled pack, so any pack-id-keyed response would fail. The lab_schema_tag() helper reads the tag from the source file at test time rather than comparing against a constant, preventing trivial injection via the report output.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: docs/compatibility-schema.md provides a normative table mapping each of the 8 output JSON fields to their type and precise meaning (e.g., link_band: string, 'tight' when no corrupt rows remain and echo plus queue checks pass; otherwise 'noisy'). The instruction explicitly points agents to this document. The schema is authoritative and complete, not merely an example.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The Dockerfile pins the base image to a full SHA-256 digest, pins each apt package to an exact version (e.g., cmake=3.25.1-1, g++=4:12.2.0-3), pins uv to 0.9.5 via its versioned install URL, and pins Python packages to exact versions (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5). Pinning is thorough across all dependency layers.
✅ pass - typos: All file paths referenced in instruction.md, the Dockerfile, CMakeLists.txt, and tests are consistent with the actual directory structure and file names. Environment variable names (PLC_BRIDGE_PACK, PLC_BRIDGE_OUT) are spelled identically in the instruction, the test file, and the source code. No mismatched identifiers, command names, or path components were found.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile's COPY instructions enumerate only specific environment subdirectories (cmd, wire, seq, timing, safety, ledger, sim, metrics, config, util, decoy, data, catalog, deploy, docs, ci, bench, include). Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is copied into the image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh rewrites each buggy C++ source file (op_a.cpp, op_b.cpp, op_c.cpp, op_d.cpp, op_e.cpp) with corrected implementations using heredocs, then runs cmake to configure and build, installs the binary, and executes it — exactly the steps an agent would perform. No field values are hardcoded; the correct output values derive from the fixed simulation running against the actual data files.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: The instruction explicitly states 'leave /app/output/compatibility_report.json matching that run', clearly naming the output file the tests look for (REPORT = OUT / 'compatibility_report.json').

The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite uses source rebuild, hidden fixtures, and diverse scenarios
that together prevent meaningful shortcuts while verifying all core algorithms.

Strengths:  Tests rebuild the binary from agent-modified source at verification
time, and two undisclosed fixture packs (inaccessible to the agent) test
generalization of every output field with exact value assertions.

Weaknesses: The safety check (op_d) is only tested for the negative case
(estop without recovery → false) and no-estop (→ true); the positive case
(estop properly followed by reset before move → true) is never exercised.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 13 pytest assertions covering all 8 report fields
across 5 input packs (3 visible, 2 hidden). The verifier rebuilds the C++
binary from source, preventing pre-compiled binary injection. Hidden
fixtures with unique field combinations (e.g., frames_recovered=2,
link_band="noisy" from queue skew) force correct algorithmic implementations
for ledger repair, timing gate, and safety checks. The diverse gap values
across packs (12ms fails, 15ms passes for arm→poll) constrain thresholds
enough that naive single-value shortcuts fail against the hidden fixtures.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Safety op_d only tested for negative/absent estop — no recovery path
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:152-216 (all fixture assertions for safe_mode)
Problem: The safety function (op_d) is tested only in two scenarios: (1) no
estop in transcript → safe_mode=true, and (2) estop followed by move without
reset → safe_mode=false. No fixture tests the case where estop IS properly
handled (estop → reset → arm → move → safe_mode=true). A naive
implementation that returns false whenever any estop opcode is present would
pass all tests.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # estop_skip: expects safe_mode=False (estop then move, no reset)         │
│  # bundled/noisy/secret1/secret2: expects safe_mode=True (no estop)        │
│  # No fixture has estop + proper reset + move → True                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Add a fixture with transcript like:                                     │
│  # [reset, arm, estop, reset, arm, move] → safe_mode=True                  │
│  # This forces the agent to track state properly rather than               │
│  # using "estop present → false" shortcut                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An agent could implement op_d as simply checking whether opcode 4
(estop) appears anywhere in the transcript and returning false if so. This
passes all current tests because no fixture combines estop presence with a
safe_mode=true expectation. The shortcut is only marginally simpler than the
correct state-tracking implementation, and the agent still needs to identify
estop as the relevant concept, so exploitation risk is low.

================================================================================

output_data.formatted_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite uses source rebuild, hidden fixtures, and diverse scenarios
that together prevent meaningful shortcuts while verifying all core algorithms.

Strengths:  Tests rebuild the binary from agent-modified source at verification
time, and two undisclosed fixture packs (inaccessible to the agent) test
generalization of every output field with exact value assertions.

Weaknesses: The safety check (op_d) is only tested for the negative case
(estop without recovery → false) and no-estop (→ true); the positive case
(estop properly followed by reset before move → true) is never exercised.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 13 pytest assertions covering all 8 report fields
across 5 input packs (3 visible, 2 hidden). The verifier rebuilds the C++
binary from source, preventing pre-compiled binary injection. Hidden
fixtures with unique field combinations (e.g., frames_recovered=2,
link_band="noisy" from queue skew) force correct algorithmic implementations
for ledger repair, timing gate, and safety checks. The diverse gap values
across packs (12ms fails, 15ms passes for arm→poll) constrain thresholds
enough that naive single-value shortcuts fail against the hidden fixtures.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Safety op_d only tested for negative/absent estop — no recovery path
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:152-216 (all fixture assertions for safe_mode)
Problem: The safety function (op_d) is tested only in two scenarios: (1) no
estop in transcript → safe_mode=true, and (2) estop followed by move without
reset → safe_mode=false. No fixture tests the case where estop IS properly
handled (estop → reset → arm → move → safe_mode=true). A naive
implementation that returns false whenever any estop opcode is present would
pass all tests.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # estop_skip: expects safe_mode=False (estop then move, no reset)         │
│  # bundled/noisy/secret1/secret2: expects safe_mode=True (no estop)        │
│  # No fixture has estop + proper reset + move → True                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Add a fixture with transcript like:                                     │
│  # [reset, arm, estop, reset, arm, move] → safe_mode=True                  │
│  # This forces the agent to track state properly rather than               │
│  # using "estop present → false" shortcut                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An agent could implement op_d as simply checking whether opcode 4
(estop) appears anywhere in the transcript and returning false if so. This
passes all current tests because no fixture combines estop presence with a
safe_mode=true expectation. The shortcut is only marginally simpler than the
correct state-tracking implementation, and the agent still needs to identify
estop as the relevant concept, so exploitation risk is low.

================================================================================

Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

## Job Run Summary

### 1. Overall Results
- **0/3 trials passed** — all scored reward=0
- Reward distribution: `0, 0, null`
- Verifier ran on **2/3 trials** (tbench-task__CAUvrR4, tbench-task__3UyLMWY); tbench-task__jVhi4Ap never reached the repair phase before a tmux server crash terminated the session

---

### 2. Common Failure Patterns
Both agent-run trials reached **exactly 13/14 tests (92.8%)** before failing on a single fixture — a striking convergence. In both cases, the primary fix was logically sound, but introduced or left unresolved a secondary condition:

- **tbench-task__CAUvrR4**: Agent correctly fixed 4 bugs but made an architectural decision to exclude the reset→arm edge from `timing_ok` checks (reasoning it was covered by `handshake_ok`). This caused the `noisy_gap` fixture to return `timing_ok=True` instead of the expected `False`.
- **tbench-task__3UyLMWY**: Fixing `ledger/op_e.cpp` inadvertently flipped `sequence_ok` from `False→True` for the `estop_skip` fixture (a regression), and `safe_mode=False` was never addressed for that fixture.

**Pattern**: Both failures were "last-mile" errors — correct primary repairs with undetected side effects on adjacent fixtures, not caught because agents didn't fully re-validate all fixture fields after patching.

---

### 3. Hack Check
- ✅ **No cheating detected** across all 3 trials — all `reward_hacking` checks passed
- All `task_specification` checks also passed
- Agents worked legitimately: reading source, writing fixes, rebuilding, running the verifier

---

### 4. Debug / Spec Issues
- **No systematic spec or instruction issues** identified
- tbench-task__jVhi4Ap's failure was purely an **infrastructure issue** (tmux server crash after 4 steps), not a spec gap — the agent had correctly identified the build system and 4 broken output fields before the crash
- No shared misunderstanding of task requirements observed across trials

---

### 5. Progress (Failed Trials)
| Trial | Progress | Failure Type |
|---|---|---|
| tbench-task__CAUvrR4 | 13/14 (92.8%) | Wrong architectural logic on timing edge |
| tbench-task__3UyLMWY | 13/14 (92.8%) | Regression + missed field in estop fixture |
| tbench-task__jVhi4Ap | 0/14 (0%) | Infrastructure failure (no repairs attempted) |

**Average meaningful progress (excluding infra failure): 92.8%** — both agents were one test away from a perfect score. The core competency (diagnosing and patching complex C++ bugs) is working; the gap is in post-fix cross-fixture validation.

The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

Reviewing task: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task (skill: terminus-2nd-ed-task-reviewer)
Review output: ================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to debug and repair a C++ PLC bridge that drives
an industrial line controller via RS-485 protocol. The broken sources contain
five distinct bugs: wire codec ignoring revision bytes, missing state-transition
guards, hardcoded timing thresholds instead of policy lookups, a no-op safety
module, and a no-op ledger repair function. The agent must reverse-engineer
correct behavior from captures, catalog policy files, and replay packs, then
fix the C++ sources so the rebuilt binary produces correct compatibility
reports for both disclosed and undisclosed fixture packs. The test suite
verifies all 8 report fields across the bundled session pack, two known
fixtures, and two hidden grading fixtures—rebuilding from the agent's source
code each time to prevent shortcut answers.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Oracle Solution Missing Directory Creation for /app/bin
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/solution/solve.sh (line 139)
Problem: The solution runs `install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/
         plc_bridge` but the Dockerfile never creates the /app/bin/ directory.
         GNU `install` without the `-D` flag does not create parent
         directories, so the oracle would fail when run standalone.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/plc_bridge                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  mkdir -p /app/bin                                                          │
│  install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/plc_bridge                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The test suite independently creates the directory via Python's
`Path.mkdir(parents=True)` in `rebuild_bridge()`, so grading still works. But
the oracle solution should also work when executed directly to validate task
solvability. The same issue exists in `environment/ci/preflight.sh`.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Use a More Descriptive Task Directory Name
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/ (directory name)

Current approach: The task directory is named "tbench-task" which is generic
and does not convey what the task is about.

Suggested improvement: Use a domain-descriptive kebab-case name such as
`plc-bridge-rs485-repair` or `legacy-plc-wire-compat`.

Rationale: A descriptive directory name helps with task discovery, cataloging,
and distinguishing this task from others in a task suite.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an exceptionally well-designed hard debugging task that requires deep
C++ comprehension, protocol reverse-engineering, and state-machine reasoning
across five independent subsystems. The anti-cheating measures are strong:
tests rebuild from source and grade against hidden fixtures.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Five distinct, interrelated bugs requiring independent diagnosis — each
    tied to a different protocol subsystem (codec, sequencer, timing, safety,
    ledger)
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: tests always rebuild from agent-modified source and
    include undisclosed grading fixtures that test generalization
  ✓ Comprehensive 14-test suite with excellent docstrings, full schema
    validation, and cross-fixture integrity checks

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Oracle solution would fail standalone due to missing /app/bin directory
    creation (tests unaffected)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, protocol reverse-engineering, state-machine analysis,
           reading policy/config files to infer correct behavior

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Add `mkdir -p /app/bin` before the install command in both solve.sh and
  ci/preflight.sh. Once fixed, the task is ready to use — all other aspects
  (structure, tests, Dockerfile, anti-cheating) are excellent.
================================================================================

output_data.review:
================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to debug and repair a C++ PLC bridge that drives
an industrial line controller via RS-485 protocol. The broken sources contain
five distinct bugs: wire codec ignoring revision bytes, missing state-transition
guards, hardcoded timing thresholds instead of policy lookups, a no-op safety
module, and a no-op ledger repair function. The agent must reverse-engineer
correct behavior from captures, catalog policy files, and replay packs, then
fix the C++ sources so the rebuilt binary produces correct compatibility
reports for both disclosed and undisclosed fixture packs. The test suite
verifies all 8 report fields across the bundled session pack, two known
fixtures, and two hidden grading fixtures—rebuilding from the agent's source
code each time to prevent shortcut answers.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Oracle Solution Missing Directory Creation for /app/bin
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/solution/solve.sh (line 139)
Problem: The solution runs `install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/
         plc_bridge` but the Dockerfile never creates the /app/bin/ directory.
         GNU `install` without the `-D` flag does not create parent
         directories, so the oracle would fail when run standalone.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/plc_bridge                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  mkdir -p /app/bin                                                          │
│  install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/plc_bridge                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The test suite independently creates the directory via Python's
`Path.mkdir(parents=True)` in `rebuild_bridge()`, so grading still works. But
the oracle solution should also work when executed directly to validate task
solvability. The same issue exists in `environment/ci/preflight.sh`.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Use a More Descriptive Task Directory Name
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/ (directory name)

Current approach: The task directory is named "tbench-task" which is generic
and does not convey what the task is about.

Suggested improvement: Use a domain-descriptive kebab-case name such as
`plc-bridge-rs485-repair` or `legacy-plc-wire-compat`.

Rationale: A descriptive directory name helps with task discovery, cataloging,
and distinguishing this task from others in a task suite.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an exceptionally well-designed hard debugging task that requires deep
C++ comprehension, protocol reverse-engineering, and state-machine reasoning
across five independent subsystems. The anti-cheating measures are strong:
tests rebuild from source and grade against hidden fixtures.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Five distinct, interrelated bugs requiring independent diagnosis — each
    tied to a different protocol subsystem (codec, sequencer, timing, safety,
    ledger)
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: tests always rebuild from agent-modified source and
    include undisclosed grading fixtures that test generalization
  ✓ Comprehensive 14-test suite with excellent docstrings, full schema
    validation, and cross-fixture integrity checks

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Oracle solution would fail standalone due to missing /app/bin directory
    creation (tests unaffected)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, protocol reverse-engineering, state-machine analysis,
           reading policy/config files to infer correct behavior

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Add `mkdir -p /app/bin` before the install command in both solve.sh and
  ci/preflight.sh. Once fixed, the task is ready to use — all other aspects
  (structure, tests, Dockerfile, anti-cheating) are excellent.
================================================================================

The original equipment vendor is gone and the shop-floor HMI must drive the line controller again using only RS-485 captures under `/app/data/captures/`, partial tables in `/app/data/firmware/`, and replay packs. Recent sessions show handshake stalls after reset, transcript rejects on motion-class steps, gap faults between command classes, and estop transitions that leave the arm path unsafe even though maintenance logs stayed green.

Repair the C++ bridge sources under `/app` (entry in `/app/cmd/plc_bridge`), reconcile behavior with `/app/catalog/` policy tables, rebuild `/app/bin/plc_bridge`, run it on `/app/data/session_pack.json`, and leave `/app/output/compatibility_report.json` matching that run. Optional env `PLC_BRIDGE_PACK` selects the replay JSON; `PLC_BRIDGE_OUT` selects the report directory. `/app/docs/compatibility-schema.md` defines schema_tag, link_band, handshake_ok, frames_recovered, sequence_ok, timing_ok, safe_mode, and restore_kind. Fixture packs under `/app/data/fixtures/` must generalize; grading substitutes packs including undisclosed packs supplied only at verify time. Each pack must recompute every outward field from simulation, not from pack_id shortcuts.

Replay every JSON under `/app/data/fixtures/` in addition to the bundled session pack; `/app/ci/preflight.sh` runs the same smoke replays. Signal completion once the bundled report shows corrected service.

Reviewing task: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task (skill: terminus-2nd-ed-task-reviewer)
Review output: ================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to debug and repair a C++ PLC bridge that drives
an industrial line controller via RS-485 protocol. The broken sources contain
five distinct bugs: wire codec ignoring revision bytes, missing state-transition
guards, hardcoded timing thresholds instead of policy lookups, a no-op safety
module, and a no-op ledger repair function. The agent must reverse-engineer
correct behavior from captures, catalog policy files, and replay packs, then
fix the C++ sources so the rebuilt binary produces correct compatibility
reports for both disclosed and undisclosed fixture packs. The test suite
verifies all 8 report fields across the bundled session pack, two known
fixtures, and two hidden grading fixtures—rebuilding from the agent's source
code each time to prevent shortcut answers.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Oracle Solution Missing Directory Creation for /app/bin
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/solution/solve.sh (line 139)
Problem: The solution runs `install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/
         plc_bridge` but the Dockerfile never creates the /app/bin/ directory.
         GNU `install` without the `-D` flag does not create parent
         directories, so the oracle would fail when run standalone.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/plc_bridge                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  mkdir -p /app/bin                                                          │
│  install -m 0755 /app/build/plc_bridge /app/bin/plc_bridge                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The test suite independently creates the directory via Python's
`Path.mkdir(parents=True)` in `rebuild_bridge()`, so grading still works. But
the oracle solution should also work when executed directly to validate task
solvability. The same issue exists in `environment/ci/preflight.sh`.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Use a More Descriptive Task Directory Name
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/ (directory name)

Current approach: The task directory is named "tbench-task" which is generic
and does not convey what the task is about.

Suggested improvement: Use a domain-descriptive kebab-case name such as
`plc-bridge-rs485-repair` or `legacy-plc-wire-compat`.

Rationale: A descriptive directory name helps with task discovery, cataloging,
and distinguishing this task from others in a task suite.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an exceptionally well-designed hard debugging task that requires deep
C++ comprehension, protocol reverse-engineering, and state-machine reasoning
across five independent subsystems. The anti-cheating measures are strong:
tests rebuild from source and grade against hidden fixtures.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Five distinct, interrelated bugs requiring independent diagnosis — each
    tied to a different protocol subsystem (codec, sequencer, timing, safety,
    ledger)
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: tests always rebuild from agent-modified source and
    include undisclosed grading fixtures that test generalization
  ✓ Comprehensive 14-test suite with excellent docstrings, full schema
    validation, and cross-fixture integrity checks

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Oracle solution would fail standalone due to missing /app/bin directory
    creation (tests unaffected)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, protocol reverse-engineering, state-machine analysis,
           reading policy/config files to infer correct behavior

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Add `mkdir -p /app/bin` before the install command in both solve.sh and
  ci/preflight.sh. Once fixed, the task is ready to use — all other aspects
  (structure, tests, Dockerfile, anti-cheating) are excellent.
================================================================================

4- Really good review - Nice feedback, all comments are accurate and provided a clear way to fix the task. The reviewer also identified some issues that were not spotted by our evals

5- Stellar review - The submitter should've been able to fix the task based on the comments
